topics.title: Unit Testing with JUnit 5 & Mockito
topics.description: JUnit 5 is the standard Java testing framework with @Test, @BeforeEach, @ParameterizedTest and assertions (assertEquals, assertThrows, assertAll). Mockito creates test doubles: @Mock (verify interactions), @Spy (partial mock), @Captor (capture args). The key principle: unit test in isolation — mock all external dependencies (DB, HTTP, filesystem). Integration tests use @SpringBootTest with real context. TDD: Red → Green → Refactor.
topics.story: Every production bug has two victims: the code and the absent test that should have caught it. Unit testing is insurance: cheap upfront, saves catastrophic losses later. Mockito is the stunt double: when your service calls the database, Mockito plays the database — same interface, zero I/O. Your service doesn't know it's performing to an empty theatre. This isolation is the point: test EXACTLY one thing, remove all other variables.
topics.analogy: Unit test = testing a car's brake pedal in isolation on a test bench (no engine, no wheels). Integration test = test driving the full car on a closed track. E2E test = real road trip. @Mock = stunt double (same interface, fake behavior). @Spy = real actor who you can intercept. @Captor = hidden microphone to record what arguments the actor was given. AAA pattern = Arrange (set up the scene), Act (call the method), Assert (verify the outcome).
topics.memoryAnchor: @Test: test method. @BeforeEach/@AfterEach: setup/teardown. @DisplayName: readable test name. @ParameterizedTest + @ValueSource/@CsvSource: data-driven. @ExtendWith(MockitoExtension.class): Mockito in JUnit 5. @Mock: creates mock. @InjectMocks: creates class under test, injects mocks. when(mock.method()).thenReturn(value). doThrow(ex).when(mock).method(). verify(mock, times(1)).method(). assertThrows(Ex.class, () -> ...). assertAll for grouped assertions.
topics.firstPrinciples: A unit test is a small, fast, isolated test of one unit of behavior. 'Isolated' means no real DB, no real HTTP, no real filesystem — replace with fast fakes (mocks). Fast tests run in CI on every commit. Slow tests run less often and provide less feedback. Test behavior, not implementation: tests should pass when you refactor the internals without changing the contract.
topics.bruteForce: No tests: deploy, find bugs in production, fix, redeploy. Manual testing: slow, doesn't scale, same bugs reappear. Over-coupled tests: test touches DB, runs in 5 seconds, breaks on schema change, fails in CI because no DB. These are the patterns that make teams hate testing.
topics.optimizedApproach: Unit test: mock all I/O, sub-millisecond per test. 500 tests run in 2 seconds. Integration test: @SpringBootTest + H2 in-memory, runs in 5-10 seconds, tests the full stack. E2E test: Selenium/Playwright, runs in minutes, tests user flows. Pyramid: many unit tests, fewer integration, few E2E.
topics.whenToUse: Unit test: business logic, calculations, transformations, validations. Integration test: repository queries against real DB schema, Spring Security config, REST endpoint contract. E2E: critical user journeys (login, checkout). Mock: external services (payment, email, HTTP). Don't mock: domain objects, value objects, utility methods (those are implementation details).

examples.title: JUnit 5 Assertions & Parameterized Tests
examples.explanation: @BeforeEach creates a fresh Calculator before each test — no shared state between tests. @ParameterizedTest + @CsvSource runs the same test with multiple input/output combinations — DRY test data. assertAll reports ALL failures in the group, not just the first. assertThrows verifies both the exception type AND message. @DisplayName makes test output readable for non-developers.
examples.realWorldUse: DevLearner: test TopicService.getByCategory(), test LearningGateService.computeStage(), test JwtService.validate(). @CsvSource for different role/permission combinations. assertThrows for invalid input validation. Always test happy path + edge cases + error cases.

examples.title: Mockito — Mock, Verify & Capture
examples.explanation: @Mock creates a fake of the interface — no real DB, no real email. @InjectMocks creates UserService and injects the mocks automatically. when().thenReturn() stubs the mock's behavior. verify() confirms the mock was called correctly. ArgumentCaptor captures the actual argument passed to save() or sendWelcome() so you can assert on it. verify(mock, never()) confirms a method was NOT called — critical for testing that no side effects happen on error paths.
examples.realWorldUse: Test Spring @Service classes in isolation: @Mock JpaRepository, @Mock EmailService, @InjectMocks UserService. Spring Boot: @MockBean replaces a real bean in the Spring context for @SpringBootTest. Verify email is sent exactly once on registration. Verify audit log is written only on successful operations. Capture and assert on the exact SQL arguments or JPA entities saved.

problems.title: Write Tests for a BankAccount Class
problems.description: Write JUnit 5 tests for a BankAccount with deposit(double), withdraw(double), and getBalance(). Test: normal operations, overdraft throws IllegalStateException, deposit of negative amount throws IllegalArgumentException, initial balance is 0.
problems.hint1: Four test methods: testDeposit, testWithdraw, testOverdraft, testNegativeDeposit.

problems.title: Mock an Email Service
problems.description: A UserRegistrationService calls UserRepository.save() and EmailService.sendVerification(email). Write a Mockito test that: verifies save() is called once, verifies sendVerification() is called with the correct email, and verifies no interaction with EmailService when save() throws an exception.
problems.hint2: Use verify(repo, times(1)).save(any()) and ArgumentCaptor for email.

problems.title: Parameterized Test for Input Validation
problems.description: Write a @ParameterizedTest for a `validate(String email)` method that returns true for valid emails and false for invalid. Test cases: 'alice@example.com'→true, 'bob@test.co.uk'→true, 'invalid'→false, ''→false, null→false, '@nodomain'→false.
problems.hint1: Use @CsvSource with null represented as empty string or @NullSource.
problems.hint2: The validate method can use a regex or simple contains('@') check.
problems.hint3: @NullAndEmptySource covers both null and empty string cases.
